era_context: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor is at the height of its power, with the capital recently moved to Beijing and the Forbidden City under construction. The Hussite Wars are in their seventh year following Jan Hus's execution. The Ottoman Empire is in transition, with Murad II recently ascending to the throne. The Renaissance is beginning to emerge in Italy. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Empire is consolidating power under its leadership.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas beyond Aztec and Inca heartlands

events.title: Murad II ascends to Ottoman throne
events.description: Murad II ascended to the Ottoman throne in 1421, following the death or abdication of his predecessor. Murad II's reign (1421–1444, 1446–1451) marked a period of Ottoman consolidation and expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans. He faced challenges from rival powers and internal factions, but he successfully established Ottoman dominance in the region. Murad II's reign saw the development of Ottoman administrative structures and the integration of conquered territories. He was known for his military prowess and his ability to navigate complex political situations. His reign set the stage for the later Ottoman expansion under his successors, particularly the conquest of Constantinople in 1453.
events.certainty_note: Murad II's ascension to the Ottoman throne in 1421 is well-documented in Ottoman chronicles and later historiographic accounts.

events.title: Forbidden City under construction; Beijing becomes Ming capital
events.description: In 1421, the Forbidden City was under construction in Beijing, which had recently become the capital of the Ming Dynasty. The Yongle Emperor had moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing in 1421, marking a major shift in the center of Ming power. The construction of the Forbidden City was one of the greatest architectural projects of the medieval world, requiring the labor of hundreds of thousands of workers. The move to Beijing was motivated by strategic considerations, as the city was closer to the northern frontier and better positioned to defend against Mongol invasions. The construction of the Forbidden City and the move to Beijing represented the consolidation of Ming power and the establishment of Beijing as one of the great capitals of the world.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The move of the capital to Beijing and the construction of the Forbidden City are well-documented in Ming records and confirmed by archaeological evidence.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Jan Žižka's military campaigns continue
events.description: In 1421, Jan Žižka continued his military campaigns in Bohemia, consolidating Hussite control and defending against Catholic crusades. Žižka's innovative tactics, particularly the use of wagon fortifications (Wagenburg), made Hussite armies formidable opponents. In 1421, Žižka was engaged in campaigns against both Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. His military successes strengthened the Hussite position in Bohemia and made him a legendary figure in Czech history. The Hussite Wars represented one of the earliest challenges to papal authority and foreshadowed the later Protestant Reformation.
events.certainty_note: Jan Žižka's military campaigns in 1421 are documented in contemporary Hussite chronicles and later historiographic accounts.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue; Ming maritime dominance
events.description: In 1421, Zheng He's treasure voyages were in their second decade, continuing to project Ming power and prestige across the Indian Ocean. The voyages brought back exotic goods, tribute from foreign rulers, and diplomatic recognition of Ming supremacy. Zheng He's fleets visited ports in East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Southeast Asia, establishing trade networks and collecting tribute. These voyages represented the peak of Ming maritime exploration and were supported by the Yongle Emperor, who viewed them as demonstrations of imperial power and legitimacy. The voyages were economically costly and were eventually discontinued after the Yongle Emperor's death.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ma Huan's Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The continuation of Zheng He's voyages in 1421 is confirmed by Ming records, though the exact itineraries and dates of individual voyages are sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Early Italian Renaissance: Humanist scholars and artists active
events.description: In 1421, the Italian Renaissance was in its early stages, with humanist scholars and artists beginning to revive classical learning and artistic techniques. Florence was emerging as a center of cultural innovation, with figures like Cosimo de' Medici beginning to patronize artists and scholars. The rediscovery of classical texts and the study of ancient Greek and Roman civilization were reshaping European intellectual life. In 1421, artists and scholars were beginning to develop new techniques and ideas that would define the Renaissance. The Renaissance represented a gradual shift from medieval to early modern European culture, with roots extending back to the 13th and 14th centuries.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (Florentine banker and patron)

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (later compilation)
Surviving artworks and manuscripts from the period

events.certainty_note: The early Renaissance in Italy is well-documented through surviving artworks, manuscripts, and later biographical accounts. The dating of specific cultural developments is sometimes approximate.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1421 as a turning point in world history is debatable. While the move of the Ming capital to Beijing and the construction of the Forbidden City were major events, some scholars argue that these developments did not represent a fundamental shift in Ming power or prestige, as the empire had already been dominant for decades. The dating of the move to Beijing is sometimes given as 1420 or 1421, with some sources suggesting the move was gradual rather than sudden. Murad II's ascension to the Ottoman throne in 1421 is well-documented, but the significance of this event is sometimes overstated; Ottoman expansion was a gradual process that had been underway for decades. The Italian Renaissance is often portrayed as a sudden cultural flowering, but modern scholarship emphasizes the gradual continuity between medieval and Renaissance culture. The Hussite Wars are sometimes portrayed as a major challenge to papal authority, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Hussite movement was ultimately suppressed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1421 is dominated by East Asian and European sources, with rich documentation of political, military, and cultural events. The Ming Dynasty is well-documented in court records and later historiographic accounts, though the perspective is heavily weighted toward elite male actors and state-level politics. The move to Beijing and the construction of the Forbidden City are confirmed by both documentary and archaeological evidence. The Hussite Wars are documented in contemporary chronicles, but the perspective is often biased toward either the Hussite or Catholic side. The Italian Renaissance is known through surviving artworks and later biographical accounts, which may emphasize certain figures and developments over others. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Pacific Islands, and interior Americas remain virtually absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often produced by elites and may not reflect the experiences of common people or marginalized groups.

graph_edges.note: Murad II's ascension in 1421 set the stage for Ottoman expansion that culminated in the fall of Constantinople in 1453.
The move to Beijing and construction of the Forbidden City in 1421 were part of the Yongle Emperor's broader program of imperial consolidation that began after his ascension in 1402.
The Hussite Wars in 1421 were a direct result of Jan Hus's execution in 1415.
Zheng He's voyages in 1421 were part of the larger series of treasure voyages that began in 1405.
The early Renaissance cultural developments in 1421 contributed to the flourishing of Italian art and humanism in subsequent decades.
